title: Annattha (The Permitted Exceptions in Kāyotsarga)
summary: Annattha is the short formula a practitioner recites just before standing (or sitting) in kāyotsarga — the meditative 'abandonment of the body' in which one stays motionless and silent. Because a living body cannot help breathing, coughing, or stirring slightly, this sutra names the small, involuntary acts that are *allowed* and do not break the vow of stillness. It then declares that, apart from these exceptions, the practitioner will keep the body steady, the mouth silent, and the mind in meditation until the kāyotsarga is formally ended by saluting the Arihantas. In short, it sets the honest, livable conditions of the practice so that the kāyotsarga remains 'unbroken and unviolated.'
ritualPlacement: Annattha belongs to the fifth Āvaśyaka, Kāussagga (kāyotsarga). It is recited as the second half of a fixed pair: first Tassa Uttari (the resolve to purify oneself through kāyotsarga), then immediately Annattha (the exceptions that frame that kāyotsarga). The silent kāyotsarga that follows is measured in Logassa-recitations and is concluded by the Namaskāra (Navkar). Like most core Āvaśyaka sutras, Annattha is *shared*: the identical text is used in all five pratikramaṇas (Devasi, Rāi, Pakkhī, Cāumāsī, Sāṁvatsarī). Note one subtlety stated here once: the well-known pakkhī→cāumāsī→sāṁvatsarī word-substitution does **not** alter Annattha's wording (the word 'pakkhī' does not occur in it); what changes between observances is only the *length* of the kāyotsarga it introduces (e.g. 12 Logassa for Pakkhī, 20 for Cāumāsī, 40 + 1 Navkar for Sāṁvatsarī).
authorAndSource: — No individual author. Annattha is a traditional Ardhamāgadhī Prakrit formula of the Āvaśyaka tradition, of ancient and anonymous canonical redaction, transmitted as a fixed liturgical pair with Tassa Uttari. In this corpus the authoritative reading is the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) edition of *Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit*; the text is to be preserved verbatim, including its orthography.
structureArc: The five verses move from concrete exceptions to a formal vow. (1) lists the gross involuntary acts that are permitted — out-breath and in-breath, coughing, sneezing, yawning, belching, breaking wind, dizziness, and fainting from bile. (2) adds the three *subtle stirrings* (suhuma saṁcāla): of the limbs, of phlegm, and of the eyes/gaze. (3) closes the list with 'evamāiehiṁ āgārehiṁ' — 'by these and similar exceptions' — and states the goal: that the kāyotsarga be abhagga (unbroken) and avirāhia (unviolated). (4) opens the temporal clause 'jāva … na pāremi' — 'as long as I have not concluded (it) by the Namaskāra to the Arihanta Bhagavants.' (5) completes the sentence with 'tāva …' — 'until then I abandon (vosirāmi) my body' — by ṭhāṇa (steady posture), moṇa (silence), and jhāṇa (meditation). Verses 4–5 are thus a single jāva…tāva ('as long as … until then') sentence that defines exactly when the kāyotsarga begins and ends.
recurringImagery: There is no pictorial metaphor here; the sutra's unifying device is grammatical and conceptual. First, the **āgāra** ('permitted reservation') — the legal-spiritual idea that a vow can name its own honest exceptions so that unavoidable bodily reflexes do not count as a breach. This single concept governs all of verses 1–3. Second, the **jāva…tāva** frame ('as long as … until then') of verses 4–5, a conditional bracket that fixes the duration of the vow: the kāyotsarga is in force from the moment of declaration *until* it is released by the Namaskāra. Recognizing these two devices once is enough to read the whole sutra; the per-verse notes need only point to them.
keyThemes: kāyotsarga — meditative abandonment of bodily concern
āgāra — honest, permitted exceptions that keep a vow livable
the body's involuntary acts vs. willed movement
subtle (sūkṣma) stirrings of limb, phlegm, and gaze
the temporal bracket of the vow (jāva…tāva), ended by the Namaskāra
ṭhāṇa–moṇa–jhāṇa: stilling body, speech, and mind

glossary.definition: 'except for / otherwise than' — the opening word that introduces the list of permitted exceptions.
'abandonment of the body'; standing or sitting motionless and silent in meditation, the fifth Āvaśyaka.
a permitted exception or reservation built into a vow; an involuntary act that does not break it.
breathing out / breathing in — the most basic permitted reflexes.
fainting or swoon caused by (a disturbance of) bile.
subtle, very slight — qualifying the barely perceptible stirrings that are excused.
a slight movement or stirring; here of the limbs (aṅga), of phlegm (khela), and of the sight/eyes (diṭṭhi).
unbroken — said of a kāyotsarga that has not been interrupted.
unviolated, undamaged — free of fault or transgression.
'as long as … until then' — the conditional pair that brackets the duration of the vow (vv. 4–5).
the salutation (Navkar) to the Arihantas by which the kāyotsarga is formally concluded (pāremi).
by steady posture, by silence, and by meditation — the three means by which the body is 'set aside.'
'I abandon / give up' — the operative verb of renunciation, here of attachment to the body.

howToRead: A newcomer can read Annattha simply as the 'rules of stillness' — a kind permission slip that lists the natural bodily acts you needn't worry about, followed by your promise to stay still, silent, and meditative until the closing salutation. An advanced practitioner will attend to the jāva…tāva structure binding verses 4–5 into one vow-sentence, and to the precise scope of the āgāra (gross reflexes in v.1, the three subtle saṁcāla in v.2, and the open-ended 'evamāiehiṁ' in v.3).

sources.title: Kayotsarga

sources.title: Pratikramana

notes: Authority for every reading, mātrā, anusvāra, and conjunct is the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) book *Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit*; the verses above are honored verbatim and not normalized. Verses 1–2 explicitly name twelve permitted acts (nine gross reflexes + three subtle saṁcāla); the phrase 'evamāiehiṁ āgārehiṁ' ('by these and similar exceptions') in v.3 extends the list to further unavoidable acts. Several Śvetāmbara teaching traditions count a total of sixteen āgāra by adding emergency exceptions (e.g. fire, flood, a creature or collapsing object) drawn from the wider Cheda-sūtra tradition; this expanded count is a traditional gloss whose exact membership varies by sect and is not enumerated in the sutra text itself — treat it as tradition-dependent. The English titles 'breaking wind / belching' for uḍḍueṇaṁ and vāyanisaggeṇaṁ follow the standard understanding (belch; release of bodily gas); minor variation in such glosses exists across translators.
